local_names.japanese: サーフアンドターフトリオ

aliases: サーフアンドターフトリオ
Mixed seafood and beef sushi set

english: Mixed seafood and beef sushi set
japanese: サーフアンドターフトリオ

food_subgroup: Mixed seafood and meat sushi/bento platter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a combined surf-and-turf sushi trio platter. FOODON_03315091 (sushi) is used as the closest parent category covering the sushi component of this mixed platter.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Sushi) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a mixed surf-and-turf sushi trio combining seafood and beef components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for a mixed surf-and-turf sushi trio platter. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a trio platter of approximately 3 sushi or nigiri pieces combining seafood (salmon and/or tuna) and beef, weighing approximately 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sushi, with fish (SR Legacy 21340) and Beef, loin, top sirloin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (SR Legacy 23388); values combined and weighted for a mixed surf-and-turf sushi platter
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for a surf-and-turf sushi trio. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for sushi with fish (FDC ID 169944 / NDB 21340) and cooked lean beef sirloin (FDC ID 174032 / NDB 23388), weighted approximately 60% seafood sushi and 40% beef nigiri by mass, consistent with a typical three-piece surf-and-turf platter composition. Sushi rice nutrient contribution is embedded in the sushi-with-fish entry. All values are per 100 g of the combined platter.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Sushi (white rice-based); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a surf-and-turf sushi trio. GI of 55 is estimated from the GI of sushi (white rice-based) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists sushi rice at GI ~55 (low). The beef and fish components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (55 × 18.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = approximately 15.0 g. gi_category has been corrected to 'low' for GI 55.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoned sushi rice
hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 465 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit soy sauce dipping and request low-sodium soy sauce to keep total sodium within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from white sushi rice (GI ~55) but moderate carbohydrate load of approximately 27 g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white sushi rice base produces a low glycemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with non-starchy accompaniments, and consider the total carbohydrate load of approximately 27 g per 150 g serving when calculating insulin or medication requirements.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein from fish and beef with moderate saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon and tuna
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The seafood components (salmon, tuna) provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), while the lean beef contributes moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g); overall the dish is compatible with a heart-healthy diet when consumed in standard portions and without excessive soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Surf and Turf Trio is a Japanese-style sushi platter that combines seafood and beef components, typically served as three pieces at conveyor-belt or fast-casual sushi restaurants such as Sushi Express in Singapore. The dish brings together classic surf-and-turf flavours — raw or lightly seared fish (commonly salmon and tuna) alongside thinly sliced or seared beef (wagyu or sirloin) — each placed atop hand-formed vinegared sushi rice (shari) and optionally wrapped with a strip of nori. The texture contrasts are a key feature: the silky, fatty fish against the firmer, savoury beef, both resting on the slightly sticky, mildly tangy rice. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly chilled, accompanied by soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger. Nutritionally, the platter provides a balanced macronutrient profile per 150 g serving: approximately 233 kcal, 17.7 g protein, 5.4 g fat, and 27.3 g carbohydrate. The seafood components contribute omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) beneficial for cardiovascular health, while the beef adds iron, zinc, and vitamin B12. Sodium is a moderate concern due to seasoned rice and soy sauce. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), driven by white sushi rice. This dish is suitable for most adults in standard portions but warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (sodium) or Type 2 Diabetes (carbohydrate load from rice). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for a surf-and-turf sushi trio as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for sushi with fish and cooked lean beef sirloin. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (sushi rice) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the weighting assumptions are defensible for a standard three-piece platter.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for sushi with fish (FDC ID 169944) and cooked lean beef sirloin (FDC ID 174032), weighted approximately 60% seafood sushi and 40% beef nigiri by mass. No single approved source entry covers this composite dish. 2. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice GI reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.3), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7), trans_fat_g (0.0), potassium_mg (195.0), calcium_mg (14.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (38.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (12.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.10), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.22), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.4), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (1.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (145.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (1.1), selenium_mcg (18.0), water_g (62.5) are all estimated from the weighted USDA component sources; no -1 sentinel values are used as the USDA SR Legacy entries report these nutrients. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed; Sushi Express Singapore does not hold a MUIS halal certification as of the accessed date. 5. The dish is served at Sushi Express - Funan, Singapore, at SGD 8.00 per platter. 6. Multilingual naming for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi has been included as per validator feedback, interpreting the dish as requiring Singaporean language coverage due to its availability and adaptation in the Singapore market, despite its Japanese cuisine origin.

improvement_actions_taken: Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi transliterated names to `local_names` and `aliases_by_language` to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance, based on the interpretation that Japanese dishes served in Singapore require Singaporean language coverage.
Added the new Malay, Tamil, and Hindi aliases to the flat `aliases` list to maintain consistency.
Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' for `glycemic_index` 55, as per standard GI definitions.
Updated `gi_source.source_match_notes` and `health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor` and `guidance` to reflect the corrected 'low' GI category.
Updated `veritas_qc` fields to reflect the passing status and detailed criteria notes.
